summary: O'Neil argues that the reproduction rate R(t) is a more reliable metric for tracking Covid-19 than raw case counts or model predictions. She explains that R(t) can be estimated from multiple data sources (hospitalizations, cases, deaths) and is robust to testing biases since it only measures trends. However, she warns that deliberate political manipulation of data by states eager to reopen undermines even this approach. The piece advocates for following this single hyperparameter rather than getting lost in complex model outputs.
key_points: R(t) can be triangulated from multiple data sources, creating a more trustworthy composite picture than any single metric
The reproduction rate is insulated from testing bias because it tracks trends rather than absolute numbers
Several states (Texas, Florida, Georgia) have manipulated Covid data to justify reopening prematurely
Following a hyperparameter like R(t) is more practical than trusting complex model predictions
money_quotes: R(t), which is a hyperparameter in most Covid-19 models, is a much better and more trustworthy figure to follow than any other particular data set.
We can piece together a trustworthy patchwork quilt of R(t)'s that might be more trustworthy than any particular version.
R(t) is insulated from the bias we know exists in these figures (due mostly to not enough tests) and only cares about trends, so as long as the bias is consistent we don't care about it.
We've seen many states performing Covid-19 data manipulation (Texas, Florida, and Georgia for example) in order to open up sooner than they honestly should.
Basically, they're juicing the numbers. That's a kind of political bias we cannot overcome easily.
key_insight: When data quality is suspect, tracking the trend-based reproduction rate R(t) across multiple sources is more reliable than trusting any single dataset or complex model.
